card.intuition: A segment tree over x-coordinates where each node stores the line that is dominant at the node's midpoint. Inserting a new line: compare at midpoint, keep the winner at this node, and recurse with the loser into the half where it might still win. Query at point x: check all O(log n) ancestors.
card.analogy: A tournament bracket where lines compete at each round's midpoint: the winner of that round is recorded, but the loser gets another chance in a sub-bracket where they might dominate. The overall minimum at any x is the minimum among all recorded winners on the path to x.
card.time_complexity: O(log C) per line insert; O(log C) per point query — C = coordinate range
card.space_complexity: O(C) implicit tree or O(q log C) with dynamic nodes
when_to_use: Convex hull trick when lines and queries arrive in arbitrary order (no monotonicity needed)
Range minimum of linear functions over a set of lines
DP with lines of arbitrary slope order
gotchas: Li Chao tree is built over x-coordinate range, not line count — size depends on query domain
Only stores ONE dominant line per node — but correctly handles queries via ancestor path
Supports only minimum (or maximum) queries — not arbitrary aggregates

exercise.prompt: Implement a Li Chao tree for minimum line queries over integer x-coordinates.
exercise.solution:
line, lo, mid
line, mid+1, hi
hints: Recurse with the loser line into the left half if left_better != mid_better (loser might win on the left)
Otherwise recurse into the right half (loser might win on the right)
exercise.test_code:
lct = LiChaoTree(0, 10)
lct.add((2, 0))    # y = 2x
lct.add((1, 3))    # y = x + 3
lct.add((0, 10))   # y = 10
assert lct.query(0) == 0    # y=2*0=0 wins
assert lct.query(3) == 6    # y=2*3=6, y=1*3+3=6 tie
assert lct.query(4) == 7    # y=1*4+3=7 wins over 2*4=8
assert lct.query(8) == 10   # y=10 wins over 2*8=16, 1*8+3=11
print('PASS')
